perturbation.original_text: 2.0 RECIPIENT agrees that for a period of two years from the date of receipt of the information, it shall not disclose or duplicate any information it receives from OWNER that is marked or designated as CONFIDENTIAL, PROPRIETARY or STRICTLY PRIVATE (hereinafter collectively referred to as "PROPRIETARY") to any other person (including RECIPIENT'S employees in any other Group or entity), firm, or company, or use it for its own benefit, except as provided herein.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.0 RECIPIENT agrees that for a period of one year from the date of receipt of the information, it shall not disclose or duplicate any information it receives from OWNER that is marked or designated as CONFIDENTIAL, PROPRIETARY or STRICTLY PRIVATE (hereinafter collectively referred to as "PROPRIETARY") to any other person (including RECIPIENT'S employees in any other Group or entity), firm, or company, or use it for its own benefit, except as provided herein.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the non-disclosure period from 'two years' to 'one year' creates a contradiction. Later clauses assuming a two-year protection period are now invalidated for information received after the first year from the agreement date. For example, any legal action taken after one year but before two years, relying on the two-year confidentiality, now becomes disputable due to this conflicting definition. The in-text contradiction affects the enforcement of confidentiality obligations beyond one year.
perturbation.contradicted_text: RECIPIENT agrees that for a period of one year from the date of receipt of the information, it shall not disclose or duplicate any information it receives from OWNER that is marked or designated as CONFIDENTIAL, PROPRIETARY or STRICTLY PRIVATE (hereinafter collectively referred to as "PROPRIETARY") to any other person (including RECIPIENT'S employees in any other Group or entity), firm, or company, or use it for its own benefit, except as provided herein.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the non-disclosure period from two years to one year. The contradicted text states 'RECIPIENT agrees that for a period of one year from the date of receipt of the information'. Although they state the same period now, the explanation highlights that later clauses may assume a two-year protection period based on the original text, leading to a contradiction for information received after the first year. This makes the changed text incompatible with other parts of the agreement.

perturbation.original_text: 5.0 The parties hereto agree that information shall not be deemed PROPRIETARY and RECIPIENT shall have no obligation with respect to, any such information which; i is already known to RECIPIENT; ii is or becomes publicly known through no wrongful act of RECIPIENT or any employee or RECIPIENT; (iii is rightfully received from a third party without restriction and without breach of this Agreement; iv is independently developed by RECIPIENT without breach of this Agreement; v is furnished to a third party by OWNER without a similar restriction on third party's rights; vi is approved for release by written authorisation of OWNER; viii. is disclosed pursuant to the requirement or request of a Governmental agency or where disclosure is required by operation of law.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.0 The parties hereto agree that information shall not be deemed PROPRIETARY and RECIPIENT shall have no obligation with respect to, any such information which; i is already known to RECIPIENT before the signature of this agreement; ii is or becomes publicly known through the RECIPIENT actions or any employee or RECIPIENT; (iii is rightfully received from a third party with restriction and with breach of this Agreement; iv is independently developed by RECIPIENT without breach of this Agreement; v is furnished to a third party by OWNER with a similar restriction on third party's rights; vi is approved for release by written authorisation of OWNER; viii. is disclosed pursuant to the requirement or request of a Governmental agency or where disclosure is required by operation of law.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the conditions under which information is 'not deemed PROPRIETARY' introduces conflicting definitions. Specifically, changing 'is already known to RECIPIENT' to 'is already known to RECIPIENT before the signature of this agreement' limits the scope of prior knowledge that exempts information. Also 'ii is or becomes publicly known through no wrongful act of RECIPIENT or any employee or RECIPIENT' to 'ii is or becomes publicly known through the RECIPIENT actions or any employee or RECIPIENT' creates liability. The condition 'iii is rightfully received from a third party without restriction and without breach of this Agreement' to 'iii is rightfully received from a third party with restriction and with breach of this Agreement'. These alterations create an in-text contradiction. Clauses defining appropriate data handling now contradict this revised definition. For example, if RECIPIENT already knew the information, but after the agreement signature, it could be considered a breach based on other clauses regarding proprietary information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The parties hereto agree that information shall not be deemed PROPRIETARY and RECIPIENT shall have no obligation with respect to, any such information which; i is already known to RECIPIENT before the signature of this agreement; ii is or becomes publicly known through the RECIPIENT actions or any employee or RECIPIENT; (iii is rightfully received from a third party with restriction and with breach of this Agreement; iv is independently developed by RECIPIENT without breach of this Agreement; v is furnished to a third party by OWNER with a similar restriction on third party's rights; vi is approved for release by written authorisation of OWNER; viii. is disclosed pursuant to the requirement or request of a Governmental agency or where disclosure is required by operation of law.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the conditions under which information is deemed 'PROPRIETARY', introducing direct contradictions. Specifically, limiting prior knowledge to 'before the signature of this agreement' conflicts with a broader understanding of prior knowledge. Additionally, altering 'no wrongful act of RECIPIENT' to 'RECIPIENT actions' shifts responsibility. The changed condition 'iii is rightfully received from a third party without restriction and without breach of this Agreement' to 'iii is rightfully received from a third party with restriction and with breach of this Agreement' introduces a contradiction because it now suggests proprietary information can be received with restrictions but still be considered non-proprietary under this clause. This directly contradicts the original intent of the clause, making its application inconsistent and therefore creating a strong in-text contradiction as it changes the obligations of the recipient.

perturbation.original_text: 6.0 The parties hereto further agree that RECIPIENT shall not be liable for: i. inadvertent disclosure or use of PROPRIETARY information provided that a. it uses the same prudence as set forth above, in safeguarding such PROPRIETARY information, and b. upon discovery of such inadvertent disclosure or use of such PROPRIETARY information, it endeavours to prevent any further inadvertent disclosure or use, 1 ii. unauthorised disclosure or use of PROPRIETARY information by persons who are or have been in its employ, unless it fails to prudently safeguard such PROPREETARY information.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.0 The parties hereto further agree that RECIPIENT shall be liable for: i. inadvertent disclosure or use of PROPRIETARY information provided that a. it uses the same prudence as set forth above, in safeguarding such PROPRIETARY information, and b. upon discovery of such inadvertent disclosure or use of such PROPRIETARY information, it endeavours to prevent any further inadvertent disclosure or use, 1 ii. unauthorised disclosure or use of PROPRIETARY information by persons who are or have been in its employ, unless it fails to prudently safeguard such PROPREETARY information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not be liable' to 'shall be liable' for inadvertent disclosure creates a direct contradiction. Previous clauses outlining the protection of proprietary information are now undermined, as the RECIPIENT is now liable regardless of their efforts to protect the data, which is an in-text contradiction. For example, any situation described in section 5.0 where information is not 'deemed PROPRIETARY', if RECIPIENT discloses it, RECIPIENT will be liable for.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The parties hereto further agree that RECIPIENT shall be liable for: i. inadvertent disclosure or use of PROPRIETARY information provided that a. it uses the same prudence as set forth above, in safeguarding such PROPRIETARY information, and b. upon discovery of such inadvertent disclosure or use of such PROPRIETARY information, it endeavours to prevent any further inadvertent disclosure or use, 1 ii. unauthorised disclosure or use of PROPRIETARY information by persons who are or have been in its employ, unless it fails to prudently safeguard such PROPREETARY information.
perturbation.justification: The original text states the RECIPIENT 'shall not be liable' for inadvertent disclosure under certain conditions. The changed text states the RECIPIENT 'shall be liable' for inadvertent disclosure under the same conditions. This directly contradicts the original agreement by reversing the liability, thus creating a clear in-text contradiction between the liability and lack of liability, that causes ambiguity, uncertainty, inconsistency, and potential unenforceability.

perturbation.original_text: 8.0 The parties hereto understand that each may currently or in the future be developing PROPRIETARY information internally, or receiving PROPRIETARY information from third parties which may be similar to disclosing party's information. Accordingly, nothing in this Agreement shall be construed as a representation or inference that each party hereto will not develop products, for itself or for others, that compete with the products, systems or methods contemplated by disclosing party's information.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.0 The parties hereto understand that each may currently or in the future be developing PROPRIETARY information internally, or receiving PROPRIETARY information from third parties which may be similar to disclosing party's information. Accordingly, this Agreement shall be construed as a representation or inference that each party hereto will not develop products, for itself or for others, that compete with the products, systems or methods contemplated by disclosing party's information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'nothing in this Agreement shall be construed as a representation or inference that' phrase fundamentally changes the clause's meaning. Now, the agreement implies that parties *cannot* develop competing products, contradicting the overall intention of free operation and the exception clauses. An example of in-text contradiction will be on '5.0 The parties hereto agree that information shall not be deemed PROPRIETARY', which limits the agreement by allowing the RECIPIENT to use this for own benefit as long as it is not PROPRIETARY, but under clause 8.0 as perturbed, creates an ambiguity on how to use that data or what to do with that data.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The parties hereto understand that each may currently or in the future be developing PROPRIETARY information internally, or receiving PROPRIETARY information from third parties which may be similar to disclosing party's information. Accordingly, this Agreement shall be construed as a representation or inference that each party hereto will not develop products, for itself or for others, that compete with the products, systems or methods contemplated by disclosing party's information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes 'nothing in this Agreement shall be construed as a representation or inference that,' fundamentally altering the clause's meaning. Now, the agreement implies that parties cannot develop competing products, contradicting the original intention and creating an ambiguity. Clause '5.0 The parties hereto agree that information shall not be deemed PROPRIETARY' contradicts the new interpretation of clause 8.0 because clause 5.0 gives the RECIPIENT the right to use information that is not PROPRIETARY, but modified 8.0 says that parties cannot develop competing products, creating a conflict on whether the RECIPIENT can even use that information.

perturbation.original_text: 9.0 Each party hereto respectively appoints the below identified person as its Data Control Coordinator for the receipt and dispatch, on its behalf, of all PROPRIETARY information it discloses to the other party hereto pursuant to this Agreement. Each party reserves the right to change its Data Control Co-ordinator by giving the other party written notice thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: 9.0 Each party hereto respectively appoints the below identified person as its Data Control Coordinator for the receipt and dispatch, on its behalf, of all PROPRIETARY information it discloses to the other party hereto pursuant to this Agreement. Each party can never change its Data Control Co-ordinator.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the ability to change the Data Control Coordinator creates a contradiction with the implied ability to adapt and the potential need for changes over time. Other sections that imply ongoing communication with the Data Control Coordinator now have a permanent, fixed point of contact, even if that contact becomes impossible or obsolete. For example, the clause 10, related with oral disclosure, now the coodinator can't be changed, creating a contradiction if that coordinator can't longer perform his task due to a reason.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each party hereto respectively appoints the below identified person as its Data Control Coordinator for the receipt and dispatch, on its behalf, of all PROPRIETARY information it discloses to the other party hereto pursuant to this Agreement. Each party can never change its Data Control Co-ordinator.
perturbation.justification: The changed text states that a party 'can never change its Data Control Co-ordinator', while the original text allowed for changes with written notice. This directly contradicts the original functionality and creates an in-text contradiction. The explanation highlights that the implication of 'ongoing communication' is now disrupted because the coordinator cannot be changed even if they are unable to perform their task, leading to a clear conflict. The contradicted text is the same as the changed text, highlighting that the contradiction is internal and definitive.

perturbation.original_text: 11.0 All written PROPRIETARY information delivered by OWNER to RECIPIENT pursuant to this Agreement and any authorised copies thereof shall remain the property of OWNER, and shall be promptly returned to OWNER upon written request.
perturbation.changed_text: 11.0 All written PROPRIETARY information delivered by OWNER to RECIPIENT pursuant to this Agreement and any authorised copies thereof shall remain the property of RECIPIENT, and shall be promptly returned to OWNER upon written request.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'property of OWNER' to 'property of RECIPIENT' directly contradicts the fundamental understanding of ownership and confidentiality established earlier in the agreement. If the information is now the RECIPIENT's property, the restrictions on use and disclosure become significantly weakened. Example: Clause 2.0 now becomes self-contradictory, since the RECIPIENT owns the PROPRIETARY information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: All written PROPRIETARY information delivered by OWNER to RECIPIENT pursuant to this Agreement and any authorised copies thereof shall remain the property of RECIPIENT, and shall be promptly returned to OWNER upon written request.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the ownership of the PROPRIETARY information from the OWNER to the RECIPIENT. This directly contradicts the fundamental understanding of ownership and confidentiality, as now the RECIPIENT owns the PROPRIETARY information. This contradiction triggers the condition described in the explanation, which says 'If the information is now the RECIPIENT's property, the restrictions on use and disclosure become significantly weakened. Example: Clause 2.0 now becomes self-contradictory, since the RECIPIENT owns the PROPRIETARY information.'. Thus, the changed text creates a clear and direct conflict with the contradicted text.
